title: 1.1: Constructs three test packets with explicit code: a baseline Ether/IP/TCP (e.g., `Ether()/IP()/TCP()`), one with short payload (~10 bytes, e.g., `Raw(load=b'A'*10)`), and one with unknown EtherType (e.g., `Ether(type=0x9000)`).
1.2: Reports concrete raw byte length measurements (e.g., 54 bytes for Ether/IP/TCP) and explicitly references or compares against the `conf.min_pkt_size` configuration variable (default 60 bytes).
1.3: Observes that test packets have no automatic Padding layer in memory after construction or after Ether(raw(packet)) reconstruction.
1.4: Documents the payload size threshold where packets reach the minimum frame size (e.g., 6-byte payload yields 60-byte packet).
1.5: Identifies where Scapy decides to add padding, including the padding mechanism (e.g., in scapy/arch/linux.py send function using conf.min_pkt_size - len(x)).
1.6: Details that the Ethernet layer uses EtherType-to-protocol bindings to determine the next layer (e.g., 0x0800 maps to IP, 0x0806 maps to ARP).
1.7: Shows that packets with unrecognized EtherType decode to Raw data after the Ethernet layer (e.g., [Ether, Raw] structure).
1.8: Explains the fallback logic when EtherType is not recognized (e.g., guess_payload_class checks bindings, then default_payload_class returns Raw).
1.9: Displays packet structure for at least one test packet (e.g., output from pkt.show() or print(pkt)).
2.1: Claims that Padding layer behavior or dissection varies by operating system (e.g., Windows handles padding differently than Linux).
